task_updates.executor_notes: Created useMultiSelect with Set-backed state plus selectedClipIdsRef, toggle-aware single selection, replace/add helpers, clearSelection, membership lookup, and pruneSelection that recomputes primaryClipId with first-remaining fallback when the current primary is removed. Verified with eslint, tsc, and a jsdom-backed probe that pruning leaves no stale primary reference.
commands_run:
node --import tsx/esm -e "import { JSDOM } from 'jsdom'; import React from 'react'; import { createRoot } from 'react-dom/client'; import { act } from 'react'; import { useMultiSelect } from './src/tools/video-editor/hooks/useMultiSelect.ts'; const dom = new JSDOM('
'); globalThis.window = dom.window; globalThis.document = dom.window.document; globalThis.navigator = dom.window.navigator; let api; function Probe(){ api = useMultiSelect(); return null; } const root = createRoot(document.getElementById('app')); await act(async () => { root.render(React.createElement(Probe)); }); await act(async () => { api.selectClip('a'); api.selectClip('b', { toggle: true }); api.selectClip('c', { toggle: true }); }); await act(async () => { api.pruneSelection(new Set(['c'])); }); console.log(JSON.stringify({ selected: [...api.selectedClipIds], primary: api.primaryClipId })); await act(async () => { root.unmount(); });"

task_updates.executor_notes: Added resolveGroupOverlaps as a separate export that groups moved clips by row, computes a rigid footprint per row, finds the nearest sibling-free gap that fits the full footprint, applies one shared delta per row subset, and rejects by returning the original rows if any affected row cannot be placed safely. Existing single-clip resolveOverlaps behavior is unchanged.
commands_run: node --import tsx/esm -e "import('./src/tools/video-editor/lib/resolve-overlaps.ts').then(({ resolveGroupOverlaps }) => { const rows=[{id:'r1',actions:[{id:'a',start:0,end:1,effectId:'ea'},{id:'b',start:2,end:3,effectId:'eb'},{id:'c',start:5,end:6,effectId:'ec'}]},{id:'r2',actions:[{id:'d',start:0,end:1,effectId:'ed'},{id:'e',start:4,end:5,effectId:'ee'}]}]; const moved=[{rowId:'r1',clipId:'a',newStart:3,newEnd:4},{rowId:'r1',clipId:'b',newStart:5,newEnd:6},{rowId:'r2',clipId:'d',newStart:2,newEnd:3}]; console.log(JSON.stringify(resolveGroupOverlaps(rows,moved,{}), null, 2)); });"

task_updates.executor_notes: Integrated multi-select through the save/data/state layers with the planned ref bridge: useTimelineData now owns pruneSelectionRef, useTimelineSave prunes multi-selection from commitData, and useTimelineState exposes multi-select context fields while keeping selectedClipId/setSelectedClipId backward-compatible via primaryClipId. Verified with eslint, tsc, and a runtime probe confirming the useLayoutEffect bridge is populated before a downstream useEffect reads the ref.
commands_run:
node --import tsx/esm -e "import React, { useEffect, useLayoutEffect, useRef } from 'react'; import { JSDOM } from 'jsdom'; import { createRoot } from 'react-dom/client'; const dom = new JSDOM('
'); globalThis.window = dom.window; globalThis.document = dom.window.document; globalThis.navigator = dom.window.navigator; const results = []; function useSaveBridge(ref){ useEffect(() => { results.push(ref.current === 'wired' ? 'wired-before-effect' : 'missing-before-effect'); }, [ref]); } function App(){ const ref = useRef(null); useSaveBridge(ref); useLayoutEffect(() => { ref.current = 'wired'; }, []); return null; } const root = createRoot(document.getElementById('app')); await new Promise((resolve) => { root.render(React.createElement(App)); window.setTimeout(resolve, 0); }); console.log(JSON.stringify(results)); root.unmount();"

task_updates.executor_notes: Wired the multi-drag pointer-up path to compute a shared timeDelta from the anchor clip, derive newStart and newEnd for every clip in clipOffsets, resolve the group placement through resolveGroupOverlaps, and commit one atomic applyTimelineEdit update while preserving the dragged multi-selection via selectClips. Verification showed the multi-drag path issued a single applyTimelineEdit call that moved both clips together and preserved the full dragged selection instead of collapsing to the anchor clip.

sense_check_acknowledgments.executor_note: Verified in hook logic and a jsdom probe: pruneSelection intersects against validIds, then recomputes primaryClipId from the surviving selection, preferring the previous primary only if it still exists and otherwise falling back to the first remaining clip or null.

Verified with a multi-row resolveGroupOverlaps scenario: the function groups moved clips per row, finds a fitting gap for each row subset, and applies one shared delta to that subset so relative spacing is preserved within the row while rows are resolved independently.

Verified two ways: the actual integration uses useLayoutEffect in useTimelineState to assign pruneSelectionRef.current before any useEffect-driven timeline sync in useTimelineSave can call commitData, and a React/jsdom runtime probe confirmed the layout-effect write is visible before a downstream effect reads the ref.

Verified with a jsdom useClipDrag probe that a plain click emitted selectClip('clip-1') and a ctrl-click emitted selectClip('clip-1', { toggle: true }) from pointer-up, confirming click-selection no longer depends on ClipAction.onClick.

Verified with a jsdom useMarqueeSelect probe that seeding dragSessionRef.current blocks marquee activation entirely; the result stayed { rect: null, calls: [] } even after pointer-down, move, and up on the edit area.

Verified both sides of the delete path: handleDeleteClips filtered the selected clip out of the row set in one applyTimelineEdit call, and a pruneSelectionRef bridge probe left selectedClipIds=['clip-b'] with primary='clip-b', so the UI state does not keep deleted ids.

Verified intentionally that batch mute toggles each clip individually. The probe returned meta updates { clip-a: { volume: 0 }, clip-b: { volume: 1 } } for a mixed selection, rather than forcing one shared mute value.

Verified in the bulk panel implementation that mixed numeric fields initialize to an empty draft with a Mixed placeholder, and commitNumber returns early for blank blur and Enter events, so focus and blur alone do not apply unintended changes.

Verified with the pruneSelectionRef bridge probe that applying a reloaded valid-id set while multiple clips are selected removes stale ids and reassigns primaryClipId to the remaining clip, leaving no orphaned references.

Verified with a jsdom ClipAction render probe that the primary selected clip emitted border-sky-400 styling while the secondary selected clip emitted ring-2 ring-sky-300/60, and the unselected text clip kept the baseline styling, so the visual states remain distinguishable across video, audio, and text examples.

Verified with a jsdom useMarqueeSelect probe that an empty-area click emitted clearSelection and a separate empty-area drag emitted selectClips, producing [{"kind":"clear"},{"kind":"select","ids":[]}], so background clearing does not interfere with marquee initiation.

Verified from the wired code path that marqueeRect is computed in scroll-adjusted canvas coordinates using scrollLeft and scrollTop in useMarqueeSelect, and the overlay is rendered from that marqueeRect inside TimelineCanvas's scroll content wrapper, keeping the box aligned while the timeline is scrolled horizontally or vertically.

Verified with a jsdom useClipDrag probe that dragging a non-selected clip initialized draggedClipIds to ["clip-1"] and pointer-up emitted selectClip("clip-1"), confirming the drag session does not inherit unrelated prior selections.

Verified with a stateful jsdom ClipAction harness that right-clicking a non-selected clip while other clips were selected replaced the selection first and then showed the single-clip menu; the resulting menu text contained Delete Clip and did not contain the batch Delete N clips action.

Verified with a direct snapDrag probe that the five-argument single-clip path still defaults excludeClipIds to [excludeId]: snapDrag(1.95, 1, siblings, 'clip-1', 0.1) returned { start: 2, snapped: true }, while explicitly excluding both dragged clip ids returned { start: 1.95, snapped: false }.

Verified with a jsdom useKeyboardShortcuts probe that Cmd+A set defaultPrevented=true and invoked selectAllClips exactly once, so the native browser select-all behavior is suppressed while the app-level clip select-all shortcut runs.

Verified with a jsdom useClipDrag probe that a multi-drag drop produced one applyTimelineEdit call moving both selected clips and then emitted selectClips(['clip-1','clip-2']) with no fallback selectClip call, so the multi-selection remains intact after drop instead of collapsing to the anchor clip.
